section: Fatigue properties of Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C austenitic low-density steel: Critical impact of κ-carbide precipitation state

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the title, abstract, and Section 2.1 Materials. It establishes the investigated Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C (wt%) austenitic low-density steel, prepared by vacuum melting, forging to a 30x30 mm billet, hot rolling to 4 mm, solution treatment at 1050°C/1h (ST), and aging at 550°C, 600°C, 650°C for 6h (550-6, 600-6, 650-6). The abstract highlights that intragranular κ-carbides enhance fatigue life at low stress amplitudes via planar slip and slip reversibility, while intergranular κ-carbides reduce fatigue life at high stress amplitudes by impeding slip transmission. The study targets the critical role of κ-carbide precipitation state in tension-tension fatigue. Subsequent chunks will likely detail experimental methods, microstructures, and fatigue test results.
mechanisms: glide plane softening
planar dislocation slip
slip reversibility
slip transmission impediment
stress concentration

context_update.summary: This chunk details the materials processing (Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C, solution treated at 1050°C/1h water quenched to produce ST, then aged at 550°C/600°C/650°C for 6h to obtain 550-6, 600-6, 650-6), mechanical test parameters (tensile: room temperature, 10^-3 s^-1; fatigue: stress-controlled tension-tension, R=0.1, 20 Hz, sinusoidal, ambient air; flat dog-bone specimens, loading along rolling direction, electropolished), and microstructural characterization methods (EBSD, TEM at 200 kV, twin-jet electropolishing, post-fracture SEM/EBSD/TEM). Undeformed microstructures show a fully austenitic ST sample (Fig. 2a). The 550-6 sample contains intragranular κ-carbides of ~11 nm (rectangular); 600-6 shows ~18 nm κ-carbides aligned along <100> with observable intergranular κ; 650-6 has ~24 nm κ-carbides and more pronounced intergranular precipitation (Fig. 2, Fig. 3). Initial tensile results indicate that all aged conditions have higher yield/UTS but lower ductility; 550-6 and 600-6 are strongest, whereas 650-6 exhibits a marked drop in strength due to grain boundary weakening from intergranular κ. These microstructural and property trends set the stage for the subsequent fatigue behavior analysis, where the contrasting roles of intragranular and intergranular κ-carbides will be examined under cyclic loading.
processing_route: vacuum melting, forging, hot rolling to 4 mm
microstructure: 600-6: intragranular κ ~18 nm, aligned along <100>, intergranular κ present
650-6: intragranular κ ~24 nm, pronounced intergranular κ
properties: aged conditions: increased yield and UTS, reduced ductility
mechanisms: precipitation hardening by intragranular κ-carbides
grain boundary weakening due to serious intergranular κ-carbide precipitation

context_update.summary: This chunk presents the tensile stress–strain data (Fig. 4), fatigue properties (S-N curves, Basquin parameters, fatigue strengths at 10⁷ cycles) for ST, 550-6, 600-6, and 650-6 samples. Aged samples show longer fatigue lives than ST, with 600-6 having the highest fatigue strength at low stress amplitudes and 550-6 the longest lives at high amplitudes. Fracture analysis reveals larger crack initiation/propagation area fractions in aged samples, finer fatigue striations in 550-6/600-6, and mixed dimple+cleavage fracture in 650-6 due to intergranular κ. EBSD crack path analysis (KAM, SF) demonstrates that intragranular κ-carbides promote tortuous transgranular cracking by activating multiple slip systems and reduce grain-boundary strain localization, whereas intergranular κ in 650-6 creates KAM hot spots. This chunk provides the quantitative mechanical and fractographic evidence needed to link fatigue behavior to κ-carbide precipitation state in subsequent discussion sections.
key_variables: Stress amplitude (low vs. high, e.g., 180, 225, 315 MPa)
Fatigue life trends (600-6 optimal at low amplitudes, 550-6 at high amplitudes)
microstructure: EBSD KAM hot spots at intergranular κ-carbide grain boundaries
Crack path tortuosity and microbranching in aged samples
properties: Fatigue strength coefficient σ'_f and exponent b (Table 1)
Fatigue strength at 10⁷ cycles: ST 157.5, 550-6 162, 600-6 175.5, 650-6 171 MPa
mechanisms: Planar slip and slip reversibility (fatigue striations)
Intragranular κ strengthen and deflect cracks via multi-slip activation
Intergranular κ cause grain-boundary weakening and KAM hot spots
Strain localization differences seen by KAM
Crack nucleation in hard-oriented, propagation in soft-oriented grains
figures_or_tables: Fig. 3 κ-carbide size vs. aging temperature
Fig. 9 EBSD crack path analysis (BC, IPF, KAM, SF)

context_update.summary: This chunk covers surface deformation characteristics (SEM, Fig. 10) and deformation microstructures (TEM, Fig. 11) for ST and aged samples (550-6, 600-6, 650-6) fatigued at 180 and 315 MPa. It also discusses the impact of intragranular κ-carbides on dislocation slip mode, introducing SRO and 'glide plane softening'. ST showed wavy slip at low amplitude and kink bands at high amplitude, while 600-6 exhibited uniform planar slip bands due to reversible planar slip from κ-carbides. TEM revealed PLBs and high dislocation density in ST, versus lower density, uniformly distributed planar bands in aged samples, especially 600-6. The discussion explains how aging-induced L12 κ-carbides amplify glide plane softening via APB formation, suppress cross-slip, and enhance dislocation reversibility, reducing damage per cycle. This provides mechanistic basis for improved fatigue performance. The chunk references Fig. 10, Fig. 11, Fig. 12a, Fig. 12b.
properties: improved fatigue response due to reversible slip and reduced damage accumulation
mechanisms: glide plane softening by SRO
amplified glide plane softening by κ-carbides via APBs
enhanced slip planarity and suppressed cross-slip
dislocation reversibility

context_update.summary: This chunk covers mechanistic discussions on how intragranular and intergranular κ‑carbide precipitation states affect dislocation slip mode and fatigue behavior in Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C steel. Section 4.1 explains that aging-induced L12 intragranular κ‑carbides amplify glide plane softening beyond SRO by creating APBs, which promote homogeneous planar slip and improve dislocation reversibility under cyclic loading, reducing damage per cycle. Section 4.2 analyzes low‑stress‑amplitude fatigue: the κ‑carbides in 550‑6 (~11 nm) and 650‑6 (~24 nm) remain in the shearing regime; the strengthening contributions from coherency strain, modulus mismatch, and order strengthening are formulated. The non‑monotonic ranking 600‑6 > 650‑6 > 550‑6 > ST is explained by the trade-off between precipitate size and intergranular precipitation. Section 4.3 addresses high‑stress amplitudes: intergranular κ‑carbides in 650‑6 act as barriers, intensifying dislocation pile‑ups and leading to earlier crack nucleation, resulting in a ranking of 550‑6 > 600‑6 > 650‑6. The chunk concludes with a design guideline to promote uniform intragranular κ while suppressing grain‑boundary precipitation. Key figures are Fig. 12 (slip reversibility schematics), Fig. 13 (TEM/HRTEM of sheared κ), Fig. 14 (grain‑boundary dislocation structures), and Fig. 15 (intergranular κ effect schematic).
microstructure: tangled dislocation structures with wavy slip
mechanisms: glide plane softening via APB formation
enhanced dislocation reversibility due to slip planarity
shearing mechanism for κ < 27 nm
coherency strain, modulus mismatch, and order strengthening (Δσ_cs, Δσ_ms, Δσ_os)
intergranular κ-carbides as slip barriers causing dislocation pile-ups and early crack nucleation

section: 4.3. Effect of intergranular κ-carbides on fatigue behavior at high stress amplitudes
previous_context_summary:
Summary: 10, Fig. 11, Fig. 12a, Fig. 12b. This chunk covers mechanistic discussions on how intragranular and intergranular κ‑carbide precipitation states affect dislocation slip mode and fatigue behavior in Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C steel. Section 4.1 explains that aging-induced L12 intragranular κ‑carbides amplify glide plane softening beyond SRO by creating APBs, which promote homogeneous planar slip and improve dislocation reversibility under cyclic loading, reducing damage per cycle. Section 4.2 analyzes low‑stress‑amplitude fatigue: the κ‑carbides in 550‑6 (~11 nm) and 650‑6 (~24 nm) remain in the shearing regime; the strengthening contributions from coherency strain, modulus mismatch, and order strengthening are formulated. The non‑monotonic ranking 600‑6 > 650‑6 > 550‑6 > ST is explained by the trade-off between precipitate size and intergranular precipitation. Section 4.3 addresses high‑stress amplitudes: intergranular κ‑carbides in 650‑6 act as barriers, intensifying dislocation pile‑ups and leading to earlier crack nucleation, resulting in a ranking of 550‑6 > 600‑6 > 650‑6. The chunk concludes with a design guideline to promote uniform intragranular κ while suppressing grain‑boundary precipitation. Key figures are Fig. 12 (slip reversibility schematics), Fig. 13 (TEM/HRTEM of sheared κ), Fig. 14 (grain‑boundary dislocation structures), and Fig. 15 (intergranular κ effect schematic).

context_update.summary: This chunk presents the concluding discussion and the paper's conclusions. It emphasizes that optimal fatigue performance in Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1C low-density steel requires a uniform distribution of intragranular κ-carbides with appropriate size while suppressing detrimental intergranular κ-carbides. Intragranular κ improves low-stress-amplitude fatigue life via enhanced precipitation strengthening and slip reversibility, while intergranular κ degrades high-stress-amplitude fatigue life by accelerating stress concentration. The four conclusions summarize the key findings: planar slip occurs in both ST and aged conditions; κ-carbide strengthening follows a shearing mechanism and larger size improves low-stress fatigue; intergranular κ drastically reduces high-stress fatigue life; the 600-6 sample achieves the best overall tensile and fatigue properties. Figures 14 and 15 provide direct TEM evidence and schematic illustrations of deformation structures near grain boundaries in 550-6 and 650-6 and the effect of intergranular κ on slip transfer. This chunk finalizes the mechanistic understanding and offers microstructural design guidelines.
processing_route: Solution treatment at 1050°C for 1h, water quench
microstructure: dislocation pile-ups and intergranular κ in 650-6
properties: fatigue life at low stress amplitudes improved by intragranular κ
fatigue life at high stress amplitudes degraded by intergranular κ
mechanisms: glide plane softening enhanced by intragranular κ
slip transfer impeded by intergranular κ
